rows.v: The smallest and highest-pitched of the standard string family, and the most numerous instrument in the orchestra.
Bright, singing, and agile, with a huge expressive range from delicate whispers to soaring melody. The orchestra's main 'voice.'
Held under the chin; strings are bowed or plucked (pizzicato), with the left hand stopping notes on the fingerboard.
The soaring main melody in almost any film score; the dazzling solo in Vivaldi's 'Four Seasons.'
The alto of the string family — slightly larger than the violin, with a deeper, warmer voice.
Mellow, dark, and slightly nasal, less brilliant than the violin but rich in the middle register. Often the 'inner glue' of string harmony.
Played like a violin under the chin but larger; it reads its own clef (the alto clef) that few other instruments use.
The warm inner harmonies in Romantic symphonies; the lyrical solo in Berlioz's 'Harold in Italy.'
The tenor-bass of the strings, with one of the richest, most human-sounding voices in all of music.
Deep, warm, and resonant, capable of both a singing tenor melody and a powerful bass foundation. Often called the closest sound to the human voice.
Played seated, held between the knees with a spike (endpin) resting on the floor, bowed or plucked.
The aching solo of Elgar's Cello Concerto; the rich bass line under any string section.
The largest and lowest string instrument, anchoring the entire orchestra's harmony from the bottom.
Deep, dark, and rumbling, providing the foundation that everything else is built on. Felt as much as heard.
So large the player stands or perches on a tall stool; bowed or plucked, and a staple of jazz too.
The ominous low pulse opening many film scores; the walking bass line in jazz.
A large, triangular frame of 47 strings plucked by hand — the orchestra's most visually striking instrument.
Shimmering, ethereal, and cascading, capable of rippling glissandos that sweep across all the strings at once.
Plucked with the fingers of both hands; seven foot pedals change the pitch of the strings to play in any key.
The magical, sparkling glissando that signals a dream or flashback; Debussy and Ravel's impressionist colors.
A silver tube blown across an open hole — the highest common woodwind, and one of the oldest instrument types on Earth.
Bright, pure, and airy, agile and birdlike in its upper range, breathy and gentle lower down.
Held sideways; the player blows across the mouth hole (no reed) while fingers work the keys.
The bird calls and pastoral solos in Debussy's 'Prélude'; sparkling runs throughout the orchestra.
A tiny half-size flute that plays an octave higher — the highest-pitched instrument in the orchestra.
Brilliant, piercing, and shrill, able to cut clean through the loudest full-orchestra climax.
Played exactly like the flute but much smaller; usually doubled by a flute player switching instruments.
The whistling, soaring line on top of marches like Sousa's 'Stars and Stripes Forever.'
A double-reed woodwind with a distinctive, slightly nasal, plaintive voice.
Reedy, penetrating, and expressive, with a poignant, almost vocal quality that carries easily over the orchestra.
Sound is made by blowing through two reeds tied together that vibrate against each other.
The orchestra tunes to its steady 'A'; the haunting solo in the slow movement of many symphonies.
A single-reed woodwind with the widest range of any wind instrument and a remarkably smooth, flexible voice.
Warm and woody in its low range, bright and clear up high, capable of both jazz wail and orchestral elegance.
A single reed vibrates against the mouthpiece; fingers and keys cover the holes along the body.
The famous swooping glissando opening Gershwin's 'Rhapsody in Blue'; the heart of any jazz band.
The bass of the woodwind family — a tall double-reed instrument with a comic and a melancholy side.
Deep, reedy, and characterful; gruff and almost humorous low down, but plaintive and lyrical in its tenor range.
A long double tube folded back on itself; a curved metal tube (bocal) holds the double reed.
The lumbering grandfather theme in 'Peter and the Wolf'; the strange, high opening of Stravinsky's 'Rite of Spring.'
The English horn — a larger, lower cousin of the oboe with a deeply soulful tone (and despite the name, it's neither English nor a horn).
Mellow, melancholy, and warm, even more plaintive than the oboe, with a distinctive pear-shaped bell.
Played like the oboe with a double reed, but longer, with a bent metal crook holding the reed.
The heartbreaking 'Going Home' melody in Dvořák's 'New World' Symphony slow movement.
The highest and most brilliant of the brass family, used for fanfares, melody, and sheer power.
Bright, bold, and heroic, able to ring out triumphantly or, when muted, sound intimate and jazzy.
The player buzzes their lips into a cup mouthpiece; three valves change the length of tubing to change pitch.
Triumphant fanfares; the muted, smoky lead in a jazz combo; Aaron Copland's 'Fanfare for the Common Man.'
A coiled brass instrument with a mellow, noble voice that bridges the brass and woodwind sections.
Warm, round, and majestic, capable of soft heroic melody or powerful, ringing climaxes. Notoriously difficult to play cleanly.
The player buzzes into a funnel mouthpiece, works rotary valves with the left hand, and shapes the tone with a hand in the bell.
The sweeping, heroic main themes of John Williams scores; the noble calls in Brahms and Strauss.
A brass instrument that changes pitch with a sliding tube instead of valves — the only one to do so.
Powerful, rich, and bold, capable of majestic chorale harmony or a comic, blaring slide.
The player buzzes into the mouthpiece and moves a slide in and out to seven positions to change the pitch.
The grand chorale brass of Romantic symphonies; the smear and growl in big-band jazz.
The largest and lowest brass instrument, providing the foundation of the entire brass section.
Deep, round, and powerful, surprisingly agile and even gentle for its enormous size.
The player buzzes into a large mouthpiece; valves control its many meters of coiled tubing. Played seated, resting on the lap.
The lumbering bass under the brass section; the famous solo portraying an elephant in 'Carnival of the Animals.'
Large tunable kettledrums — the heartbeat of the orchestra and the most important pitched percussion.
Deep, resonant, and dramatic, from a soft ominous rumble to a thunderous climax. Unlike most drums, they play definite pitches.
Struck with felt-covered mallets; a foot pedal changes the tension of the drumhead to retune each drum.
The thunderous rolls and dramatic punctuation in Beethoven and Strauss; building tension in any film score.
A bright, sharp drum with wire snares stretched across the bottom head for its characteristic crisp rattle.
Crisp, cutting, and rhythmic, with a distinctive buzzing snap that drives a march or builds tension with a long roll.
Struck with two wooden sticks; the snares vibrate against the lower head to create the buzzing tone.
The driving rattle of any military march; the relentless crescendo roll in Ravel's 'Boléro.'
The largest drum in the orchestra, producing a deep, booming, pitchless thud.
Deep, booming, and powerful — felt in the chest, used for dramatic impact and thunder.
Struck with a large soft beater on a drum mounted upright; rolls use two beaters for a sustained rumble.
The cannon-like impacts in the '1812 Overture'; the thunder and dramatic accents across the orchestra.
Two large metal plates crashed together, or a single suspended cymbal struck with sticks.
Brilliant, crashing, and shimmering, from a delicate shimmer to an explosive climax.
Crash cymbals are clashed together by hand; a suspended cymbal is rolled or struck with mallets or sticks.
The shining crash at a symphony's climax; the shimmering roll building to a peak.
A set of tuned wooden bars struck with mallets, producing a bright, woody, percussive melody.
Bright, hard, and bell-like with a short, dry 'click' — distinctly woody compared to its metal cousins.
Wooden bars laid out like a piano keyboard are struck with hard mallets held in each hand.
The clattering, skeletal 'dance of the bones' in Saint-Saëns' 'Danse Macabre.'
A simple steel bar bent into a triangle — the smallest orchestral instrument with a surprisingly magical sound.
Bright, silvery, and ringing, a tiny shimmer of high sound that sparkles above the whole orchestra.
Struck with a small metal beater; the player can mute it by hand or let it ring, and trill between two sides.
The fairy-tale sparkle in Tchaikovsky's 'Nutcracker'; the famous solo in Liszt's First Piano Concerto.
A small keyboard that strikes metal plates, producing a sweet, bell-like, music-box tone.
Delicate, sparkling, and ethereal — like tiny chiming bells, gentle and otherworldly.
Looks like a small upright piano; pressing the keys makes hammers strike metal bars instead of strings.
The shimmering, magical 'Dance of the Sugar Plum Fairy' in Tchaikovsky's 'Nutcracker'; the Harry Potter theme.
A keyboard instrument that strikes strings with hammers — equally a solo star and an orchestral color.
Hugely versatile, from thunderous power to delicate intimacy, covering the full range of the orchestra by itself.
Pressing a key throws a felt hammer at the strings; pedals sustain or soften the sound. 88 keys span over seven octaves.
Every piano concerto; the percussive, driving role in Stravinsky and modern orchestral works.
A keyboard that plucks its strings rather than striking them — the keyboard of the Baroque era before the piano.
Bright, crisp, and jangly, with a distinctive metallic 'twang.' It can't play loud or soft by touch — every note is the same volume.
Pressing a key makes a small quill (plectrum) pluck the string. It has no dynamic control from finger pressure.
The continuo backbone of Bach and Vivaldi; the harpsichord sparkle throughout Baroque music.
A vast keyboard instrument that produces sound by pushing air through ranks of pipes — the largest instrument of all.
Enormous, powerful, and majestic, from a whisper to a wall of sound that can fill a cathedral and shake the floor.
Played with multiple keyboards (manuals) and a pedalboard for the feet; 'stops' select which sets of pipes sound.
The thunderous opening of Bach's 'Toccata and Fugue'; the awe-inspiring climax of Saint-Saëns' 'Organ Symphony.'
The leader who unifies the whole orchestra, shaping tempo, dynamics, and interpretation — without making a sound.
Sets the pulse, balance, and emotional shape of the music, drawing a single coherent performance from a hundred players.
Stands on a podium facing the orchestra, beating time with a baton in the right hand and shaping expression with the left.
The visible heartbeat of any live symphony; legendary figures like Bernstein, Karajan, and Dudamel.
The principal first violinist — the orchestra's second-in-command after the conductor.
Leads the string section, makes bowing decisions, and plays any violin solos. The link between players and conductor.
Enters last before the conductor, signals the oboe to sound the tuning 'A,' and leads by example from the first chair.
Walks out to applause just before the conductor; plays the violin solos within orchestral works.
The 'principal' player who leads each section and plays its solos.
Each section (oboes, cellos, trumpets, etc.) has a principal who leads its phrasing and takes the solo passages.
Sits at the front of the section, closest to the conductor, guiding entrances and the section's unified sound.
The clarinet, oboe, or horn player who stands to take a bow after a notable solo.
The largest body of the orchestra — violins, violas, cellos, and basses — forming its rich harmonic core.
The unified, blended sound of dozens of bowed strings; the warm foundation almost everything else sits on.
Divided into first and second violins, violas, cellos, and basses, often playing in lush, layered harmony.
The wall of warm sound in any film score's emotional peak; the core of the Classical and Romantic orchestra.
The arrangement of players in a fan shape around the conductor, designed for balance and blend.
Strings sit closest in front, woodwinds in the middle, brass and percussion at the back — louder instruments further away.
A traditional layout: first violins front-left, then around to cellos and basses on the right, winds behind, percussion at the rear.
The familiar curved arrangement you see at any symphony concert.
